narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family income that provide a stable foundation. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated an 82% chance that you would not pass it initially. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants most likely to succeed on the bar right away. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to peers, signaling weaker performance there relative to the typical student's mid-range standing. Your Year 1 decile was the next most influential, also at the lowest level versus the average student's stronger position, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic momentum. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, slightly below the dataset average but still competitive, actively worked in your favor by pushing the prediction toward bar exam success. Your undergraduate GPA, however, which is notably lower than the typical 3.21 average, added some weight toward the failure risk, though less than your deciles. Factors like your upper-middle family income and full-time student status provided minor positive boosts compared to the broader student distribution. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students—who often have higher decile rankings and GPAs—making the model cautious despite your personal strengths. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman leader in law; it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings and GPA, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or academic coaching tailored to law school prep. With focused improvements in those areas, you could strengthen your profile for future applications and prove the model wrong. We're rooting for you—keep pushing.
